turns.content: I'm onboarding to a new Rust codebase and I keep seeing references to implementing custom iterators with `poll_next`. Can you explain how I should go about building an async stream from scratch?

Building a custom async iterator involves wrapping your data in a type that implements the `AsyncIterator` trait. You need to implement `std::future::Future` for the iterator logic and ensure it correctly returns `Poll<Optional<Self>>`. The core of this is the `poll_next` method which needs to be implemented on your inner state struct so that Rust's async runtime knows when to yield control back.
Okay, but how do I handle the lazy evaluation part? If I just store all items in a vector and then iterate them later, isn't that not what an iterator should do?

You are correct; laziness is key. Instead of storing everything upfront, you maintain a single `state` variable inside your struct that holds the current index or data pointer. Every time `poll_next` is called, it checks if there is more work to be done and only advances the state then. This prevents loading all items into memory at once.
That makes sense for storage, but what about waking up? How does the runtime know when I'm ready to produce the next item again?

This is where waker registration comes in. You register a `Waker` with your Future's executor so that when external events happen (like data arriving or a timeout), the async runtime will call your `poll_next` method again without blocking. Think of it like setting a timer on an alarm clock; when the time hits, the system wakes you up to check if there is more work.
So I need to implement the `Waker` trait or use a specific type? And how does this integrate with the standard library's async traits?

You don't necessarily implement `Waker` directly; instead, you typically wrap your state in a struct that implements `Future` and internally manages the waker. When implementing the custom iterator, you usually create an inner type that holds the data and the waker, then expose it via a public API. The standard library's async traits like `StreamExt` or `AsyncIterator` will automatically use this waker mechanism when your future is polled.
I see. But there's one thing I'm confused about - what happens if I don't yield properly? Will the compiler just complain?

The compiler won't necessarily stop you from writing invalid logic, but it will enforce that your `Future` must be ready to run without blocking or running forever. If you forget to yield, the runtime might block indefinitely until you call `poll_next`. The key is ensuring every path in your implementation either returns a value via `Ready(Some(T))`, signals completion with `Ready(None)`, or yields control with `Poll::Pending`.
That clarifies things. But I'm still struggling to understand how the waker actually connects to my state when I implement it manually.

Imagine your iterator is a vending machine that only dispenses items one by one. The waker is like a button you press on the wall outside the machine; whenever someone presses it, the machine (your Future) wakes up and checks its internal state to see if it can give out an item next time.
Ah, okay. One more thing - what about error handling? Can I return errors from `poll_next`?

Yes, you absolutely need to handle errors in your `poll_next`. You should implement logic that returns a `Poll<Result<T>>`, where the Result contains either an item or an error type. If an error occurs during the polling process, you can propagate it and let the async runtime fail gracefully.
Great. Before I start coding, do you have any advice on structuring my state to avoid common pitfalls?

A great tip is to keep your `state` variable simple and immutable except for when you actually poll. Avoid holding references in your inner state unless absolutely necessary. Instead of storing a reference to the source data, copy the relevant fields into your own struct. This ensures your iterator remains self-contained and doesn't accidentally hold onto dead data or cause unexpected lifetimes issues.
Thanks for the advice. I think I have enough clarity now to start implementing my `poll_next` method properly.
You're very welcome! Remember to test with a simple stream first before adding complex logic. Good luck onboarding to your new project!
